articles.title: New study identifies the 3 types of masturbation habits in romantic relationships
articles.summary: A new study maps out how adults balance shared intimacy and solo sex. Based on attitudes, desire, and motives, people fall into distinct profiles that challenge common assumptions about relationships and satisfaction.
articles.aiSummary: A new study maps out how adults balance shared intimacy and solo sex. Based on attitudes, desire, and motives, people fall into distinct profiles that challenge common assumptions about relationships and satisfaction.

articles.title: Is intelligence related to happiness?
articles.summary: We often imagine the brooding genius burdened by intellect, or assume that ignorance is bliss. But what does the data say? Decades of psychological research reveal a highly complex relationship between cognitive ability, emotional skills, and human joy.
articles.aiSummary: We often imagine the brooding genius burdened by intellect, or assume that ignorance is bliss. But what does the data say? Decades of psychological research reveal a highly complex relationship between cognitive ability, emotional skills, and human joy.

articles.title: Your fingers may hold a secret of human brain evolution
articles.summary: A study of 225 newborns suggests prenatal estrogen may have played a role in the evolution of larger human brains. Boys with finger-length patterns linked to higher estrogen exposure before birth tended to have larger head circumferences, which are strongly associated with brain size. The same connection was not seen in girls.
articles.aiSummary: A study of 225 newborns suggests prenatal estrogen may have played a role in the evolution of larger human brains. articles.title: Bipolar disorder risk linked to cannabis disrupting daily routines
articles.summary: People who constantly worry about achieving happiness are more likely to use cannabis frequently and struggle to quit, according to new research. The same study explores how underlying bipolar risk relates to daily marijuana interference.

articles.title: Women, LGBTQ+ individuals, and liberals are more likely to be celebrity “stans”
articles.summary: A new study suggests that highly dedicated celebrity fans, commonly known as stans, are more likely to be female, LGBTQ+, and politically liberal. These individuals use social media to find social connection and promote their favorite public figures.
articles.aiSummary: A new study suggests that highly dedicated celebrity fans, commonly known as stans, are more likely to be female, LGBTQ+, and politically liberal. These individuals use social media to find social connection and promote their favorite public figures.

articles.title: The mysterious hum heard around the world may finally have an explanation
articles.summary: The mysterious sound known as The Hum has been reported worldwide for decades, yet its source often remains impossible to find. Researchers tested whether people who hear it have exceptional low-frequency hearing or measurable sounds produced inside their ears, but neither explanation fit most participants. The strongest possibility is that many cases involve low-frequency tinnitus, even though the sound seems completely external.

articles.title: Study finds retaliatory incivility is judged less harshly than instigatory incivility
articles.summary: When someone is rude, how we judge them depends heavily on who started it. A new study finds that people view retaliatory rudeness as more justifiable and virtuous than unprovoked rudeness, though polite responses are still preferred overall.
articles.aiSummary: When someone is rude, how we judge them depends heavily on who started it. A new study finds that people view retaliatory rudeness as more justifiable and virtuous than unprovoked rudeness, though polite responses are still preferred overall.

articles.title: Anti-democratic attitudes are highly contagious, new psychology study finds
articles.summary: The rise of anti-democratic sentiments might be driven by social contagion. Researchers have discovered that citizens often adapt their personal support for authoritarian rule to align with the political views of those around them.
articles.aiSummary: The rise of anti-democratic sentiments might be driven by social contagion. Researchers have discovered that citizens often adapt their personal support for authoritarian rule to align with the political views of those around them.

articles.title: Higher optimism is associated with a lower risk of dementia
articles.summary: A new study spanning 14 years suggests that optimism could be a powerful tool for healthy aging. Researchers found that older adults with a more positive outlook on life have a 15% lower risk of developing dementia.
articles.aiSummary: A new study spanning 14 years suggests that optimism could be a powerful tool for healthy aging. Researchers found that older adults with a more positive outlook on life have a 15% lower risk of developing dementia.

articles.title: Stanford scientists’ AI chatbot helps autistic adults improve face-to-face communication, studies show
articles.summary: For autistic adults navigating the unwritten rules of social interaction, artificial intelligence might offer a helpful practice tool. Recent research demonstrates that short sessions with a specialized chatbot can improve empathetic communication in real-world settings.
articles.aiSummary: For autistic adults navigating the unwritten rules of social interaction, artificial intelligence might offer a helpful practice tool. Recent research demonstrates that short sessions with a specialized chatbot can improve empathetic communication in real-world settings.

articles.title: Behavioral Scientist’s Summer Book List 2026
articles.summary: For your intellectual travels this summer, we’ve curated a baker’s dozen of behavioral science books newly published this year. Explore the mysteries of life and consciousness, glimpse the power of constraints and the games that define our lives, navigate life’s cliffs, and find the places that will make you who you are. — Heather Graci, Editor and Evan Nesterak, Editor-in-Chief Exploring life, death, and consciousness “Life begins, with a spark of sentience catalyzed by chemistry and chance, and then it ends, with death,” writes Maria Popova. “The great mystery is not the fable of what lies b

articles.title: Common antidepressant may ease long COVID’s crushing fatigue
articles.summary: A low-cost antidepressant may offer new hope for people struggling with long COVID fatigue. In a randomized clinical trial involving 399 adults, fluvoxamine significantly reduced fatigue and improved quality of life compared with a placebo, making it one of the first medications to show meaningful benefits for this disabling condition.

articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…an Air Traffic Controller
articles.summary: Sequencing departures like a moving puzzle, scrambling when a jet takes off without clearance, and catching what radar misses with a glance out the window with Michael Rejent, an air traffic controller. articles.title: Brain scans reveal how LSD desynchronizes local neural activity to alter consciousness
articles.summary: Brain imaging reveals that LSD works by quieting local electrical rhythms and severing small-scale synchronization in the cortex. This fragmentation forces the brain to globally integrate sensory data, fundamentally altering human consciousness and perception.
articles.aiSummary: Brain imaging reveals that LSD works by quieting local electrical rhythms and severing small-scale synchronization in the cortex. This fragmentation forces the brain to globally integrate sensory data, fundamentally altering human consciousness and perception.

articles.title: How Can We Feel Loved If We Don’t Feel Known?
articles.summary: For Sonja, researching happiness means being asked over and over again, “What is the secret to happiness?” Sonja has always dreaded that question. It’s ridiculous, it’s reductive, and it’s restrictive. But mostly she has dreaded it because she has never had a solid, science-based answer that she could offer with any confidence. There is no secret to happiness. But there are huge contributors to happiness, and some of them are more powerful than others. Sonja resists being pinned down to just one. But if she were pressed to give an answer, she now has one. She found it when she met Harry, who s

articles.title: How the brain shifts gears to appreciate the beauty of poetry
articles.summary: A new study reveals exactly what happens in the brain when we read for pleasure instead of information. By tracking blood flow, researchers found that the brain temporarily mutes word-processing regions before engaging areas linked to imagination and empathy.
articles.aiSummary: A new study reveals exactly what happens in the brain when we read for pleasure instead of information. By tracking blood flow, researchers found that the brain temporarily mutes word-processing regions before engaging areas linked to imagination and empathy.

articles.title: A tactile wearable device suggests promising results for extending total sleep time
articles.summary: A new study published in JMIR mHealth and uHealth suggests that transcutaneous vibratory stimulation can extend total sleep time. The technology provides evidence of a non-invasive option for individuals struggling to get enough sleep.
articles.aiSummary: A new study published in JMIR mHealth and uHealth suggests that transcutaneous vibratory stimulation can extend total sleep time. The technology provides evidence of a non-invasive option for individuals struggling to get enough sleep.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study published in JMIR mHealth and uHealth suggests that transcutaneous vibratory stimulation can extend total sleep time. The technology provides evidence of a non-invasive option for individuals struggling to get enough sleep.

articles.title: Do our personalities make us lonely, or does loneliness change who we are?
articles.summary: Which comes first: loneliness or a change in personality? A large new study suggests it's a two-way street. Researchers found that while being less extraverted and emotionally stable predicts future loneliness, being lonely also changes those traits over time.
articles.aiSummary: Which comes first: loneliness or a change in personality? A large new study suggests it's a two-way street. Researchers found that while being less extraverted and emotionally stable predicts future loneliness, being lonely also changes those traits over time.

articles.title: Alcohol alters a mathematical marker of brain inhibition
articles.summary: By analyzing the brain scans of intoxicated humans and rats, scientists have validated a new mathematical tool that successfully tracks the brain’s inhibitory system. This non-invasive measure could help researchers better understand various mental health conditions.
articles.aiSummary: By analyzing the brain scans of intoxicated humans and rats, scientists have validated a new mathematical tool that successfully tracks the brain’s inhibitory system. This non-invasive measure could help researchers better understand various mental health conditions.
